prompt:
A Linux workstation reports `No space left on device`. Repair the local `bin/diskpressure` workflow so it can safely diagnose and remediate the captured incident without touching the host filesystem. The repository provides a deterministic stand-in for `df`, `du`, inode statistics, logs, and deletion under `bin/diskfixture`; everything is local and no network access is needed.

First correlate block and inode utilization, `du` output, and the captured log events. `fixtures/block-pressure` is block exhaustion even though its inode headroom is healthy. `fixtures/inode-pressure` is inode exhaustion even though byte capacity and `du` totals are modest. The remediation command is intentionally limited to block-pressure incidents and must refuse to clean an inode-pressure incident.

The focused defect is in cleanup authorization. An approval names either one exact virtual path or descendants separated from that root by `/`. A similarly named sibling is never covered: approving `/var/cache/acme` must not approve `/var/cache/acme-old`. Fix that boundary check in `bin/diskpressure`. Preserve the deterministic output and fixture interfaces. During remediation, remove only candidates within an approved cache root, leave all other cache and log data intact, verify the observed block and inode changes against the metadata for the files actually removed, and write a TSV audit containing every deleted virtual path and its byte count. Do not modify `bin/diskfixture`, any fixture, or the protected test.

Useful offline commands:

    bash bin/diskpressure diagnose fixtures/block-pressure
    bash bin/diskpressure diagnose fixtures/inode-pressure
    bash tests/run.sh

The full protected regression suite must pass.

runtime_debug.baseline_evidence: Diagnosis correctly separates block pressure from inode pressure, but remediation treats an approval root as a raw string prefix and deletes `/var/cache/acme-old/do-not-delete.bin` when only `/var/cache/acme` is approved.
